subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for July 07, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - Procalcitonin to Guide 7 vs 14 Days of Antibiotics in Bloodstr
sentences: Story 1 is about Procalcitonin to Guide 7 vs 14 Days of Antibiotics in Bloodstream Infections: A Secondary Analysis of the BALANCE Trial..
This is a randomized / clinical trial from JAMA network open.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in patients were followed up for 90 days after study enrollment, how should we interpret Procalcitonin to Guide 7 compared with 14 Days of Antibiotics in Bloodstream Infections: A Secondary Analysis of the BALANCE Trial?
A total of 125 patients (median age 63, [IQR, 58-79] years; 80 [64.0%] male) were included in this study.
Sixty-five participants (52%) had low PCT levels (<250 pg/mL) and 60 (48%) had high PCT levels (≥250 pg/mL) on day 7.
Ninety-day mortality was higher in the high vs low PCT group: 21.6% (13 of 60) vs 6.2% (4 of 65) (absolute risk difference [ARD] 15.5%; 95% CI, 3.6%-27.5%).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: This may refine antibiotic selection in selected infected patients, but it should be integrated with local microbiology, renal risk, stewardship, and source control.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Continuing Vs. Withholding Home Beta-Blockers at Admission for
sentences: Story 2 is about Continuing Vs. Withholding Home Beta-Blockers at Admission for Suspected Infection: A Target Trial Emulation..
This is a recent research article from Critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
Of 4635 eligible patients, 1172 (25.3%) received an oral beta-blocker, whereas 3463 (74.7%) did not receive an oral beta-blocker.

Beta-blocker continuation was associated with a reduced risk of all-cause mortality within 90 days of hospital admission (hazard ratio 0.77; 95% CI, 0.61-0.98; p = 0.03) and shorter hospital stay (incidence rate ratio 0.39; 95% CI, 0.38-0.41; p < 0.001).
There was no significant association between beta-blocker continuation and in-hospital mortality (odds ratio 0.60; 95% CI, 0.30-1.20; p = 0.15).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: This may refine antibiotic selection in selected infected patients, but it should be integrated with local microbiology, renal risk, stewardship, and source control.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - Impact of Oxygen Targets on Sepsis Outcome: A Randomized Contr
sentences: Story 3 is about Impact of Oxygen Targets on Sepsis Outcome: A Randomized Controlled Trial..
This is a randomized / clinical trial from Shock (Augusta, Ga.).

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret ation, conventional oxygenation, and hyperoxygenation target groups, respectively, exhibiting no significant difference compared with conservative oxygen therapy, hyperoxygenation (PaO 2 : 100-150 mmHg) reduced 28-day mortality in patients with sepsis. H?

In total, 270 patients were randomly assigned to the conservative oxygenation, conventional oxygenation, and hyperoxygenation target groups (n = 86, 93, and 91), of whom 35 (40.7%), 32 (34.4%), and 17 (18.7%) died by day 28, respectively.

The 90-day mortality rates were 50.0%, 41.9%, and 36.3% in the conservative oxygenation, conventional oxygenation, and hyperoxygenation target groups, respectively, exhibiting no significant difference.
The 28-day mortality significantly differed between these groups ( P = 0.005).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Do not change practice on efficacy alone; focus journal-club discussion on whether the confidence intervals exclude a clinically important benefit or harm.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: Procalcitonin to Guide 7 vs 14 Days of Antibiotics in Bloodstream Infections: A Secondary Analysis of the BALANCE Trial.
articles.summary: The Bacteremia Antibiotic Length Actually Needed for Clinical Effectiveness (BALANCE) trial showed that 7 days of antibiotics was noninferior to 14 days among patients with bacteremia. To investigate whether an elevated serum PCT level at day 7 of bacteremia was associated with increased mortality among patients treated with 7 vs 14 days of antibiotics. This cohort study was a planned secondary analysis of the BALANCE trial from 2014 to 2023, a multicenter randomized clinical trial where serum was collected on day 7 of bacteremia and patients were followed up for 90 days after study enrollment. Serum samples were collected from patients 7 days after initiation of antibiotics; PCT levels were later quantified by an antibody-based assay and therefore were not available to clinicians. Patients were randomized to receive either 7 or 14 days of antibiotics.
articles.analysis: This may refine antibiotic selection in selected infected patients, but it should be integrated with local microbiology, renal risk, stewardship, and source control.

pico.population: patients were followed up for 90 days after study enrollment
pico.comparator: 14 Days of Antibiotics in Bloodstream Infections: A Secondary Analysis of the BALANCE Trial
pico.outcomes: The primary outcome was defined as death from any cause within 90 days of the positive index blood culture result. Secondary outcomes included death by any cause while admitted to the ICU, death by any cause while admitted to the hospital, number of days alive and not admitted to the ICU within 28 days of the index positive blood culture, number of days...

keyResults: A total of 125 patients (median age 63, [IQR, 58-79] years; 80 [64.0%] male) were included in this study.
Sixty-five participants (52%) had low PCT levels (<250 pg/mL) and 60 (48%) had high PCT levels (≥250 pg/mL) on day 7.
Ninety-day mortality was higher in the high vs low PCT group: 21.6% (13 of 60) vs 6.2% (4 of 65) (absolute risk difference [ARD] 15.5%; 95% CI, 3.6%-27.5%).
Among patients with high PCT level, 90-day mortality was not different among participants with 7 vs 14 days of antbiotics (ARD, -19.9%; 95% CI, -1.7 to 41.7).
criticalAppraisal: Interventional evidence: check allocation, blinding, protocol adherence, and absolute effect size.
Check randomization, concealment, blinding, missing data, and whether analysis was intention-to-treat.
articles.practiceImpact: This may refine antibiotic selection in selected infected patients, but it should be integrated with local microbiology, renal risk, stewardship, and source control.

phrases: This study examined whether Procalcitonin to Guide 7 versus 14 Days of Antibiotics in Bloodstream Infections.
The most clinically relevant signal was A total of 125 patients (median age 63, [IQR, 58-79] years; 80 [64.0%] male) were included in this study..
The confidence interval suggests how much uncertainty remains around the effect estimate.
For bedside practice, this matters because This may refine antibiotic selection in selected infected patients, but it should be integrated with local microbiology, renal risk, stewardship,....
I would interpret this cautiously because Check randomization, concealment, blinding, missing data, and whether analysis was intention-to-treat..

englishNotes.speakingPrompt: In one sentence, explain whether Procalcitonin to Guide 7 should change ICU practice today.

articles.title: Continuing Vs. Withholding Home Beta-Blockers at Admission for Suspected Infection: A Target Trial Emulation.
articles.summary: Patients 18 years or older on beta-blockers prior to admission hospitalized for suspected infection (defined by orders for blood cultures and broad-spectrum antibiotics). Patients with shock, heart rates less than 40 or greater than 120, or who required an IV beta- or calcium channel blocker at a clinician's discretion were excluded. Continuation of oral beta-blockers within 48 hours of admission vs. no continuation. Of 4635 eligible patients, 1172 (25.3%) received an oral beta-blocker, whereas 3463 (74.7%) did not receive an oral beta-blocker. Beta-blocker continuation was associated with a reduced risk of all-cause mortality within 90 days of hospital admission (hazard ratio 0.77; 95% CI, 0.61-0.98; p = 0.03) and shorter hospital stay (incidence rate ratio 0.39; 95% CI, 0.38-0.41; p < 0.001).

keyResults: Of 4635 eligible patients, 1172 (25.3%) received an oral beta-blocker, whereas 3463 (74.7%) did not receive an oral beta-blocker.

Beta-blocker continuation was associated with a reduced risk of all-cause mortality within 90 days of hospital admission (hazard ratio 0.77; 95% CI, 0.61-0.98; p = 0.03) and shorter hospital stay (incidence rate ratio 0.39; 95% CI, 0.38-0.41; p < 0.001).
There was no significant association between beta-blocker continuation and in-hospital mortality (odds ratio 0.60; 95% CI, 0.30-1.20; p = 0.15).
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.
Single-center data may not generalize well to different ICU resources or case-mix.

articles.title: Impact of Oxygen Targets on Sepsis Outcome: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
articles.summary: In total, 270 patients were randomly assigned to the conservative oxygenation, conventional oxygenation, and hyperoxygenation target groups (n = 86, 93, and 91), of whom 35 (40.7%), 32 (34.4%), and 17 (18.7%) died by day 28, respectively. The 90-day mortality rates were 50.0%, 41.9%, and 36.3% in the conservative oxygenation, conventional oxygenation, and hyperoxygenation target groups, respectively, exhibiting no significant difference. Sepsis is a clinical syndrome marked by a dysregulated host response to infection, impairing oxygen delivery and utilization and causing organ dysfunction. Hypoxemia, which can cause significant tissue and organ damage, commonly occurs in sepsis. Thus, oxygen therapy is essential in patients with sepsis.
articles.analysis: Do not change practice on efficacy alone; focus journal-club discussion on whether the confidence intervals exclude a clinically important benefit or harm.

The 28-day mortality significantly differed between these groups ( P = 0.005). Pairwise comparison revealed a...

keyResults: In total, 270 patients were randomly assigned to the conservative oxygenation, conventional oxygenation, and hyperoxygenation target groups (n = 86, 93, and 91), of whom 35 (40.7%), 32 (34.4%), and 17 (18.7%) died by day 28, respectively.

The 90-day mortality rates were 50.0%, 41.9%, and 36.3% in the conservative oxygenation, conventional oxygenation, and hyperoxygenation target groups, respectively, exhibiting no significant difference.
The 28-day mortality significantly differed between these groups ( P = 0.005).
Pairwise comparison revealed a significant difference between the conservative oxygenation and hyperoxygenation target groups (χ 2 = 10.132, P = 0.001).
criticalAppraisal: Interventional evidence: check allocation, blinding, protocol adherence, and absolute effect size.
Check randomization, concealment, blinding, missing data, and whether analysis was intention-to-treat.
Single-center data may not generalize well to different ICU resources or case-mix.
articles.practiceImpact: Do not change practice on efficacy alone; focus journal-club discussion on whether the confidence intervals exclude a clinically important benefit or harm.
